questions.question: What is the 'Hand-Delivery' strategy for proposals?

options.text: Physically printing the proposal and mailing it via FedEx.
Scheduling a brief 'Proposal Review' call to walk the prospect through the document before they read it alone.
Hand-delivering the proposal to the CEO's home.
Hand-writing the entire proposal on legal pads.

questions.explanation: Reviewing the proposal together ensures you maintain control of the narrative, explain the ROI logic, and address immediate questions before they focus solely on the price.

questions.question: What is a major advantage of using 'Interactive' proposal tools (like PandaDoc or Proposify) over a standard PDF?

options.text: They are always cheaper to use.
They provide real-time tracking of when the prospect opens the document and which pages they spend time on.
They automatically sign the contract for the client.
They are easier for lawyers to edit.

questions.explanation: Sales intelligence (tracking engagement) allows you to time your follow-ups perfectly and understand the prospect's hidden concerns (e.g., if they spend 10 minutes on the Terms page).

questions.question: If you see a prospect has re-opened a proposal multiple times in a single morning after weeks of silence, what does this usually indicate?

options.text: They have accidentally clicked the link multiple times.
They are likely in a meeting or actively discussing the deal internally right now.
They are trying to hack your system.
They have forgotten who you are.

questions.explanation: A sudden burst of activity usually signals internal movement. This is a high-leverage time to send a helpful, non-pushy follow-up email.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a 'Breakup' email in your follow-up cadence?

options.text: To tell the prospect you never want to speak to them again.
To professionally close the file, which often triggers a response from a prospect who was simply busy but still interested.
To complain about how much time you've wasted.
To ask for a date.

questions.explanation: Psychologically, people don't like losing access to a solution. The breakup email gives them a graceful way to either re-engage or confirm the deal is dead so you can move on.

questions.question: When should you send your first follow-up email after delivering a proposal?

options.text: One hour later.
Approximately 2 days later, to check for delivery and answer initial questions.
One month later.
Never; wait for them to contact you.

questions.explanation: 48 hours gives them enough time to review the document internally without you appearing over-eager or desperate.

questions.question: Choose a deal that recently 'ghosted' you (stopped responding after you sent a proposal). If you had known exactly which pages they read and for how long, how would your follow-up have changed?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate if the user identifies how engagement data (e.g., 'They spent 5 minutes on price but 0 on ROI') would have allowed for a more targeted, value-driven follow-up.
